companies.name: Wacker Chemie
companies.role: Produces semiconductor-grade polysilicon refined from high-purity quartz — the starting material for all DRAM wafers
companies.significance: One of the few companies capable of producing electronic-grade polysilicon at scale; expanded capacity in Burghausen and Tennessee
keyFacts: Crystal purity sets the ceiling for advanced nodes

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.role: Largest silicon wafer maker globally and key photoresist supplier — dual exposure to front-end DRAM fab
companies.significance: Dominates 300mm wafer supply alongside SUMCO; also supplies KrF/ArF photoresists used in DRAM patterning
keyFacts: Also supplies photoresists for DRAM lithography
Deep IP in crystal growth and polishing

companies.name: SUMCO
companies.role: Second-largest 300mm silicon wafer supplier — every HBM DRAM die starts on a SUMCO or Shin-Etsu wafer
companies.significance: Oligopoly position in semiconductor wafers; crystal growth and defect control are entrenched IP moats
keyFacts: Tight capacity vs rising AI wafer demand

companies.name: GlobalWafers
companies.role: Third major 300mm wafer supplier; building new U.S. capacity to localize supply for CHIPS Act fabs
companies.significance: Building Texas wafer plant to de-risk geography; wafers look like commodities but crystal growth is deep IP

companies.name: Linde
companies.role: Supplies nitrogen, argon, and neon gases used in DRAM fab purge, process, and EUV lithography
companies.significance: Industrial gases are invisible but critical — neon for excimer lasers has been a strategic pinch-point since 2022
keyFacts: Supplies N₂, Ar, Ne for semiconductor fabs

companies.name: Air Liquide
companies.role: Major supplier of ultra-high-purity process gases to DRAM and advanced packaging fabs worldwide
companies.significance: Alongside Linde and Air Products, forms the gas oligopoly that every semiconductor fab depends on
keyFacts: On-site gas plants at major foundries

companies.name: Air Products
companies.role: Supplies specialty and bulk gases including neon, nitrogen, and argon for DRAM lithography and processing
companies.significance: Part of the gas triopoly; also supplies helium and hydrogen for advanced packaging and cooling
keyFacts: Specialty gas supplier to DRAM fabs

companies.name: Entegris
companies.role: Supplies filtration systems, CMP slurries, and advanced materials that ensure nanometer-level cleanliness in DRAM fabs
companies.significance: Contamination at advanced nodes kills yields — Entegris is the clean-room gatekeeper
keyFacts: CMP slurries and filtration for DRAM

companies.name: Mitsui Mining & Smelting
companies.role: Supplies ultra-flat ED/rolled copper foils for FC-BGA substrates used in GPU+HBM packages
companies.significance: Smoother, lower-profile copper foils cut signal loss and warpage — obscure materials that dictate high-speed integrity
keyFacts: Critical for HBM package signal integrity

stages.name: Silicon & Raw Materials
stages.desc: High-purity silica, polysilicon, 300mm wafers, and the glass cloth/copper foils that form package substrates

companies.name: Ajinomoto
companies.role: Inventor and dominant supplier of Ajinomoto Build-up Film (ABF) — the dielectric layer in every high-end GPU+HBM substrate
companies.significance: Near-universal adoption at the high end; ABF is a persistent bottleneck for very large AI packages
keyFacts: De-facto standard for AI package substrates
Capacity expansion ongoing but still tight

companies.name: Unimicron
companies.role: Major FC-BGA substrate fabricator — laminates ABF onto glass cloth to build the wiring under GPU+HBM modules
companies.significance: One of the big four substrate makers adding capacity; lead times and layer counts keep rising with AI packages
keyFacts: Lamination of ABF on glass cloth

companies.name: Ibiden
companies.role: Leading FC-BGA substrate fabricator for Intel, NVIDIA, and AMD — builds the high-layer-count boards under HBM packages
companies.significance: Key substrate supplier with deep experience in high-layer FC-BGA; capacity adds ongoing but structurally tight

companies.name: Nan Ya PCB
companies.role: Fourth major FC-BGA substrate fabricator; expanding capacity to serve growing AI packaging demand
companies.significance: Part of the Formosa Plastics group; substrate business is a key growth driver as AI packages get larger

companies.name: Nittobo
companies.role: Dominant supplier of low-CTE glass cloth laminated into ABF substrates — controls thermal expansion in large AI packages
companies.significance: Glass cloth is an overlooked but critical layer; Nittobo's low-CTE material prevents warpage in enormous CoWoS packages
keyFacts: Low-CTE glass cloth for ABF substrates
Critical for thermal stability in large packages

stages.name: ABF Substrates & Packaging Materials
stages.desc: ABF film, low-CTE glass cloth, and FC-BGA substrates — the persistent bottleneck for AI-scale packages

companies.name: SK hynix
companies.role: Global leader in HBM shipments — first to qualify HBM3E with NVIDIA, now prepping HBM4
companies.significance: The HBM shipment leader; controls the most advanced DRAM stacking and TSV technology in production
keyFacts: First HBM3E qualified for NVIDIA H200

companies.name: Samsung Electronics
companies.role: Second-largest DRAM maker; improving HBM yields after a slower start, pivoting to MR-MUF bonding
companies.significance: Massive DRAM capacity but lagged in AI-grade HBM qualification; catching up with process improvements

companies.name: Micron Technology
companies.role: Clear #2 in HBM shipments; HBM3E qualified in NVIDIA H200, ramping production rapidly
companies.significance: Rose from distant third to strong #2 in HBM; highest content growth per GPU as stacks get taller

companies.name: ASML
companies.role: Sole supplier of EUV lithography systems used to pattern advanced DRAM nodes (1β/1γ) for HBM dies
companies.significance: Monopoly on EUV tools; every advanced DRAM wafer requires ASML scanners — no alternative exists

companies.name: Lam Research
companies.role: Supplies etch and deposition tools for DRAM fab and TSV drilling — critical for HBM vertical interconnects
companies.significance: TSV etch is the key enabling step for HBM stacking; Lam's tools drill the 'elevator shafts' through each die
keyFacts: TSV etch tools for HBM stacking

companies.name: Tokyo Electron
companies.role: Major supplier of etch, deposition, and coating tools for DRAM wafer fabrication
companies.significance: Alongside Lam and AMAT, forms the etch/depo triopoly that equips every DRAM fab line

companies.name: Applied Materials
companies.role: Supplies deposition, etch, and inspection tools for DRAM fab; strategic 9% stake in BESI for hybrid bonding
companies.significance: Largest WFE company; strategic investment in BESI positions it for the HBM4-era hybrid bonding transition

companies.name: KLA Corporation
companies.role: Provides wafer inspection and metrology tools that detect defects in DRAM dies before they enter HBM stacks
companies.significance: Defect detection is critical for HBM — a single bad die in a stack wastes all layers; KLA ensures Known-Good-Die

stages.name: DRAM Fabrication & TSV
stages.desc: Front-end DRAM wafer fab at 1β/1γ nodes, EUV lithography, and through-silicon via drilling for HBM stacks

companies.name: BESI
companies.role: Leads hybrid-bonding equipment for next-gen HBM4 stacking — the critical tool for denser, cooler, faster die-to-die bonds
companies.significance: AMAT took a strategic 9% stake; hybrid bonding is the key technology transition from micro-bumps to direct Cu-Cu bonds

companies.name: Henkel
companies.role: Supplies underfill epoxies and adhesives used between bonded HBM die layers to prevent warpage and voids
companies.significance: Materials chemistry that holds HBM stacks together; one of two dominant underfill suppliers alongside NAMICS

companies.name: SUSS MicroTec
companies.role: Supplies precision wafer bonders used in HBM die stacking alongside EV Group (private)
companies.significance: Precision alignment and bonding at micron scale; critical for moving from 8-Hi to 12-Hi to 16-Hi stacks

stages.name: Die Stacking & Bonding
stages.desc: Thinning, TSV reveal, micro-bump/hybrid bonding — turning individual DRAM dies into vertical HBM towers

companies.name: TSMC
companies.role: Controls CoWoS advanced packaging — the choke point that determines how many GPU+HBM modules can ship per month
companies.significance: CoWoS-L capacity is the #1 binding constraint on AI GPU throughput; TSMC doubled to ~70-75k wafers/month in 2025, still not enough
keyFacts: The tightest bottleneck in AI hardware

companies.name: ASE Technology
companies.role: Largest OSAT; expanding advanced packaging lines to absorb CoWoS overflow from TSMC
companies.significance: As TSMC's packaging hits capacity, OSATs like ASE pick up overflow — critical for scaling total AI packaging output

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.role: Building a large U.S. advanced-packaging campus to onshore capacity and deepen ties with top GPU customers
companies.significance: U.S. advanced packaging is strategic — CHIPS Act-backed, de-risks geography for the AI supply chain

stages.name: 2.5D Integration & Advanced Packaging
stages.desc: CoWoS-S/L interposers, I-Cube, EMIB — the tightest throughput constraint in AI hardware

companies.name: Advantest
companies.role: Dominates memory test equipment — HBM is a fast-growing slice of their product mix as stacks get taller and hotter
companies.significance: HBM requires more complex and earlier testing (die-level before stacking, then stack-level); Advantest tools are essential
keyFacts: Test parallelism is the next constraint

companies.name: FormFactor
companies.role: Supplies high-parallelism probe cards for wafer-level burn-in and Known-Good-Die testing of HBM DRAM
companies.significance: Every HBM stack needs KGD-tested dies to avoid wasting good layers on a bad die; FormFactor enables this at scale
keyFacts: Probe cards for HBM KGD testing

stages.name: Test & Known-Good-Die
stages.desc: HBM requires complex die-level KGD testing before stacking, then stack-level validation — insufficient test parallelism is the next constraint

companies.name: NVIDIA
companies.role: Designs the GPUs that consume HBM; delivery schedule is gated by HBM stacks and CoWoS packages, not GPU wafers
companies.significance: NVIDIA's shipment cadence is set by packaging and memory, not compute die availability — HBM is the toll booth

companies.name: AMD
companies.role: Second-largest AI GPU designer; MI300X uses HBM3 on advanced packaging, competing for the same CoWoS/HBM supply
companies.significance: Competes with NVIDIA for the same scarce HBM and packaging slots — AMD volumes add to total demand pressure
keyFacts: Competes for CoWoS and HBM supply

stages.name: GPU Integration & End Product
stages.desc: The finished GPU+HBM module — boards, power delivery, liquid cooling, and the hyperscaler buyers

companies.name: HOYA
companies.role: Dominates EUV/DUV photomask blanks — the ultra-pure glass stencils that pattern every NAND layer
companies.significance: Oligopoly with AGC in mask blanks; a single-source disruption would halt lithography across all NAND fabs

companies.name: AGC Inc.
companies.role: Second major supplier of EUV/DUV mask blanks alongside HOYA — essential for NAND patterning
companies.significance: Together with HOYA forms a duopoly in mask blanks; no alternative suppliers exist at EUV grade

companies.name: Tokyo Ohka Kogyo (TOK)
companies.role: Supplies KrF/ArF/EUV photoresists — the light-sensitive chemicals that transfer circuit patterns onto NAND wafers
companies.significance: Photoresist is concentrated in Japan; TOK, Shin-Etsu, and JSR (now private) supply virtually all advanced resists

stages.name: Quartz, Wafers & Mask Blanks
stages.desc: High-purity quartz for fused silica, photomask blanks, and 300mm wafers — the glass that starts it all

companies.name: Samsung Electronics
companies.role: Largest NAND flash producer globally; vertically integrated from wafer to finished SSD
companies.significance: Sets the pace for layer counts and bit growth; V-NAND technology leader with massive fab capacity

companies.name: Micron Technology
companies.role: Third-largest NAND producer; 232-layer and beyond NAND for enterprise SSDs
companies.significance: Only major U.S.-based NAND maker; benefits from CHIPS Act and growing AI enterprise demand

companies.name: Western Digital
companies.role: Major NAND producer via JV with Kioxia; BiCS FLASH with CBA (CMOS-bonded-to-Array) architecture
companies.significance: Kioxia/WDC JV is #2 in NAND bits; CBA bonding tech enables highest-density enterprise SSDs
keyFacts: Kioxia JV partner for NAND fab

companies.name: SK hynix
companies.role: Fourth-largest NAND producer; Solidigm subsidiary (ex-Intel NAND) adds enterprise SSD capacity
companies.significance: Solidigm acquisition gave SK hynix major enterprise SSD presence and QLC NAND leadership

companies.name: Lam Research
companies.role: Dominates high-aspect-ratio (HAR) etch tools — the most critical and time-consuming step in 3D NAND fabrication
companies.significance: As NAND adds layers, HAR etch time grows faster than bits/wafer — Lam's tools are the throughput governor
keyFacts: Dominant in HAR etch for 3D NAND
Etch time grows with layer count
The throughput governor of NAND fabs

companies.name: Tokyo Electron
companies.role: Supplies etch and deposition tools for 3D NAND film stacks, staircase etch, and wordline formation
companies.significance: Part of the etch/depo triopoly with Lam and AMAT; critical for NAND scaling beyond 200 layers

companies.name: Applied Materials
companies.role: Supplies CVD/PVD deposition tools for NAND film stacks and tungsten wordline fill
companies.significance: Tungsten fill via WF₆ CVD is a key bottleneck step; AMAT's deposition tools enable each NAND layer

companies.name: KLA Corporation
companies.role: Provides inline inspection and metrology for 3D NAND fabs — catches defects before they compound through layers
companies.significance: In 200+ layer stacks, a defect in early layers wastes all subsequent processing; KLA's tools prevent this

companies.name: ASML
companies.role: Supplies ArF immersion DUV scanners used for critical NAND patterning layers
companies.significance: NAND uses DUV (not EUV) for most layers; ASML dominates the scanner market alongside Nikon

companies.name: Nikon
companies.role: Second DUV lithography scanner supplier; re-entering with a new ArFi platform for NAND fabs
companies.significance: Provides competition to ASML in DUV scanners; new platform could gain share in cost-sensitive NAND lithography

stages.name: 3D NAND Front-End Fab
stages.desc: Film stacks, high-aspect-ratio etch, wordline deposition — building the 200+ layer 'skyscrapers' of NAND flash

companies.name: Air Products
companies.role: Supplies NF₃ and WF₆ process gases critical for NAND chamber cleaning and tungsten wordline deposition
companies.significance: NF₃ and WF₆ are in small, concentrated supply chains — a single plant incident cuts wafer throughput immediately
keyFacts: NF₃ and WF₆ for NAND fabs

companies.name: Linde
companies.role: Major supplier of bulk and specialty gases for NAND fabs including nitrogen, argon, and fluorine chemistries
companies.significance: Part of the gas triopoly; supplies the 'invisible consumables' that every NAND wafer step requires

companies.name: Air Liquide
companies.role: Supplies ultra-high-purity process gases to NAND fabs; on-site plants at major memory facilities
companies.significance: On-site gas generation at fabs ensures uninterrupted supply; critical during capacity ramp periods

companies.name: Merck KGaA / EMD
companies.role: Supplies specialty chemicals and electronic materials for semiconductor processing including WF₆ precursors
companies.significance: EMD Electronics division is a key specialty chemical supplier; concentration risk in WF₆ supply chain

companies.name: Kanto Denka Kogyo
companies.role: Key NF₃ (nitrogen trifluoride) producer for NAND fab chamber cleaning — a 2025 incident underscored concentration risk
companies.significance: NF₃ is consumed in massive quantities for chamber cleans; Kanto Denka is a concentrated single-source risk

stages.name: Specialty Gases & Chemicals
stages.desc: NF₃ for chamber cleans, WF₆ for tungsten fill — small concentrated supply chains that throttle NAND output

companies.name: Tokyo Electron
companies.role: Supplies bonding, deposition, and cleaning tools for wafer-to-wafer string stacking in high-layer NAND
companies.significance: CBA/string stacking inserts new yield modes — TEL's tools are critical for keeping bonding lines clean and precise

companies.name: SUSS MicroTec
companies.role: Supplies precision wafer bonders used in NAND string stacking and CMOS-bonded-to-Array processes
companies.significance: Wafer bonding precision determines yield for the largest-capacity NAND dies; SUSS is one of few public bonder companies
keyFacts: One of few public bonder companies

stages.name: Wafer Bonding & String Stacking
stages.desc: CBA/CuA wafer-to-wafer bonding for ultra-high-layer NAND — the yield gate for 30-60+ TB enterprise SSDs

companies.name: ASE Technology
companies.role: Largest OSAT; packages and tests NAND flash dies before they go into enterprise SSDs
companies.significance: OSAT capacity and test parallelism are hidden schedule drivers in NAND cycles

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.role: Major OSAT for NAND packaging; building U.S. capacity for memory and AI chip assembly
companies.significance: U.S.-based advanced packaging capacity is increasingly strategic for supply chain resilience

companies.name: Powertech (PTI)
companies.role: Major memory OSAT specializing in DRAM and NAND packaging — key supplier to Micron and SK hynix
companies.significance: Handles high-volume memory packaging with deep expertise in thin-die handling and stacking

companies.name: JCET Group
companies.role: Third-largest OSAT globally; acquiring Western Digital's Shanghai packaging operations
companies.significance: WD Shanghai acquisition deepens NAND packaging ties; growing player in memory assembly

companies.name: Advantest
companies.role: Dominates memory test equipment including T5835 for NAND/DRAM/HBM testing
companies.significance: Test time scales with NAND features (PLP, telemetry, QoS); Advantest order books are a leading indicator

companies.name: FormFactor
companies.role: Supplies probe cards for NAND wafer sort and Known-Good-Die testing at high parallelism
companies.significance: High-parallel probe cards scale test throughput; critical bottleneck when NAND production ramps

stages.name: OSAT Assembly & Test
stages.desc: NAND packaging, burn-in, NVMe/OCP qualification — where schedules actually slip

companies.name: Marvell Technology
companies.role: Designs Bravera SC5 enterprise SSD controllers — the SoC that orchestrates NAND, manages FTL, and speaks NVMe
companies.significance: Controller silicon is the brain of every enterprise SSD; Marvell leads in datacenter-grade controllers

companies.name: Microchip Technology
companies.role: Flashtec controller line for enterprise SSDs — competes with Marvell in high-performance NVMe controllers
companies.significance: Alternative to Marvell for enterprise SSD controllers; Flashtec platform serves hyperscaler-class drives

companies.name: Silicon Motion
companies.role: Designs MonTitan enterprise SSD controller platform — merchant controller supplier to SSD makers
companies.significance: Largest merchant SSD controller vendor; MonTitan targets enterprise/datacenter segment growing with AI

companies.name: Phison Electronics
companies.role: Designs enterprise and consumer SSD controllers; turnkey SSD solutions for OEMs who don't make their own NAND
companies.significance: Turnkey approach lets non-NAND companies enter the SSD market; growing enterprise and AI storage exposure

companies.name: Amphenol
companies.role: Supplies EDSFF E3/S connectors and high-speed interconnects used in enterprise SSD form factors
companies.significance: Form factors are shifting to EDSFF E3/S for AI racks; Amphenol connectors are the physical interface standard
keyFacts: New form factor standard for AI

companies.name: TE Connectivity
companies.role: Supplies PCIe and EDSFF connectors for enterprise SSD sleds and server backplanes
companies.significance: Alongside Amphenol, dominates the connector market for next-gen storage form factors in AI racks

stages.name: SSD Controllers & Assembly
stages.desc: Controller SoCs, DRAM cache, PLP capacitors, NVMe/PCIe interface — the 'small computer' inside each SSD

companies.name: Air Products
companies.role: Supplies argon for sputter tools coating magnetic media, plus helium for sealed nearline HDD drives
companies.significance: Argon feeds the PVD tools; helium fills drives to reduce drag — both are critical HDD inputs

companies.name: Linde
companies.role: Major supplier of argon, helium, and other process gases used in HDD media deposition and drive sealing
companies.significance: Part of the gas triopoly; helium logistics and pricing directly affect cost of high-platter-count nearline drives

companies.name: Air Liquide
companies.role: Third leg of the gas triopoly supplying argon and specialty gases for HDD media coating
companies.significance: Global gas supply network ensures HDD fabs have continuous access to ultra-pure process gases

stages.name: Minerals, Gases & Rare Earths
stages.desc: Quartz, bauxite, cobalt, platinum-group metals, rare earths (Nd/Dy), argon, and helium — the periodic table behind HDDs

companies.name: HOYA
companies.role: Dominant maker of glass substrates for HDDs — stiffer, thinner, and thermally stable vs aluminum, enabling more platters per drive
companies.significance: Glass substrates are oligopolistic; if HOYA hiccups, exabyte shipments slip across the entire HDD industry
keyFacts: Oligopolistic — no real alternative at scale

stages.name: Platters & Substrates
stages.desc: Glass and aluminum platters — what actually spins inside every nearline drive; glass enables more, thinner disks

companies.name: Resonac (Showa Denko)
companies.role: Independent anchor supplier of HDD media — co-develops next-gen HAMR FePt media with Seagate
companies.significance: Media is a first-order limiter for high capacities; FePt grain uniformity determines areal density and yields

companies.name: Canon
companies.role: Canon ANELVA division supplies PVD sputtering tools for HDD magnetic media deposition
companies.significance: Multi-chamber PVD sputtering lines are specialized capital equipment; Canon ANELVA is a dominant tool maker

companies.name: ULVAC
companies.role: Supplies vacuum deposition and sputtering equipment for HDD media coating alongside Canon ANELVA
companies.significance: Vacuum technology specialist; PVD tools for magnetic film deposition are a core product line

stages.name: Magnetic Media Deposition
stages.desc: PVD sputtering of CoCrPt / FePt magnetic films, DLC overcoats, and PFPE lube — where the bits physically live

companies.name: TDK Corporation
companies.role: Only merchant (independent) head supplier via SAE Magnetics; also supplies NdFeB magnets for actuators
companies.significance: Head yields and NFT reliability pace HAMR ramps; TDK also has magnet know-how for actuators — dual HDD exposure

companies.name: NHK Spring
companies.role: Leading supplier of precision HDD suspensions that control head flying height at nanometer altitude
companies.significance: Oligopolistic position in data-center suspensions; small geometry drifts change flying stability and scrap

stages.name: Read/Write Heads & Suspensions
stages.desc: Multilayer magnetic/photonic heads flying at nanometer gaps, plus spring-steel suspensions controlling flight height

companies.name: Nidec
companies.role: ~80% global share of HDD spindle motors — the fluid-dynamic-bearing motors that spin glass platters with minimal vibration
companies.significance: Near-monopoly position; any Nidec yield or capacity issue becomes a market-wide throttle on HDD exabyte output

companies.name: MinebeaMitsumi
companies.role: Second-largest HDD spindle motor supplier; also supplies precision bearings for various drive components
companies.significance: Only meaningful alternative to Nidec in HDD motors; runner-up position benefits when Nidec capacity is tight

stages.name: Spindle Motors & Bearings
stages.desc: Fluid-dynamic-bearing spindle motors — vibration is the enemy of areal density, and Nidec owns ~80% share

companies.name: Marvell Technology
companies.role: Supplies controller + preamp chipsets to Toshiba; read-channel SoC that decodes magnetic signals
companies.significance: New capacity points (especially HAMR) hinge on fresh channels/preamps — a tape-out delay paces a whole SKU family

companies.name: Broadcom
companies.role: Markets TrueStore read-channels and preamps for HDD OEMs — the signal processing brain of each drive
companies.significance: Read-channel/preamp silicon and firmware can pace entire SKU families; Broadcom and Marvell are the duopoly

stages.name: Electronics — Preamp, Read-Channel & SoC
stages.desc: Low-noise preamps and read-channel SoCs that turn analog magnetic whispers into digital data — new tape-outs pace SKU families

companies.name: Seagate Technology
companies.role: Leading nearline HDD OEM; ramping 30 TB-class HAMR (Mozaic 3+) drives in volume production
companies.significance: HAMR is the next areal-density leap; Seagate is first to market and sets the pricing/capacity frontier

companies.name: Western Digital
companies.role: Major nearline HDD OEM; ships 26 TB CMR and 32 TB UltraSMR ePMR drives for hyperscaler cold storage
companies.significance: ePMR/UltraSMR pathway competes with HAMR on different tech; WDC targets 40 TB+ via glass substrates

stages.name: Drive OEMs — Assembly & Final Product
stages.desc: Helium-sealed HDA assembly, servo-write, burn-in, and the three companies that ship nearline HDDs to hyperscalers

companies.name: NVIDIA
companies.role: Controls SerDes, NVLink, and GPU/switch design; also sells InfiniBand/Spectrum switches, NICs, and CPO-integrated networking.
companies.significance: NVIDIA defines the interconnect standard. NVLink and InfiniBand set the pace for AI cluster fabric.

companies.name: AMD
companies.role: GPU and CPU vendor with Infinity Fabric interconnect, driving SerDes lane counts and on-board copper complexity.
companies.significance: Infinity Fabric defines AMD's on-die and inter-chip connectivity, competing with NVLink.

companies.name: Alphabet / Google
companies.role: Designs TPU chips in-house, builds custom AI fabrics with optical circuit switching, and is a major buyer of optical transport gear.
companies.significance: Google's custom optical fabric bypasses traditional switching, pioneering OCS at datacenter scale.

companies.name: Amazon / AWS
companies.role: Hyperscaler co-developing silicon photonics with STMicro, major buyer of DCI and backbone optical systems.
companies.significance: AWS custom silicon photonics could reshape the transceiver supply chain.

companies.name: Microsoft
companies.role: Hyperscaler deploying massive GPU clusters (Azure), driving demand for 800G optics and metro DCI coherent links.
companies.significance: Azure AI cluster scale drives 800G/1.6T optics adoption and metro DCI coherent demand.

stages.name: Compute Vendors & Hyperscalers
stages.desc: The GPU/TPU designers and cloud builders driving AI networking demand

companies.name: SK Hynix
companies.role: Leading HBM manufacturer, stacked DRAM defines on-package copper fabric width; supply fully booked through 2025.
companies.significance: HBM stack height determines memory bandwidth, which defines the copper interconnect complexity.

companies.name: Samsung Electronics
companies.role: HBM and DRAM supplier, one of only three companies capable of manufacturing HBM at scale.
companies.significance: Samsung's HBM4 qualification timeline directly impacts next-gen AI networking bandwidth.

companies.name: Micron Technology
companies.role: Third HBM supplier, pre-allocated nearly all 2024-2025 output; building $9.6B HBM fab in Japan.
companies.significance: Micron's HBM4E with TSMC logic base die could change the memory-networking interface.

stages.name: HBM Memory Suppliers
stages.desc: Stacked DRAM defines on-package copper fabric width — the bandwidth wall

companies.name: TSMC
companies.role: Fabricates AI chips and provides CoWoS advanced packaging; also co-architects photonic engines and COUPE platform for CPO.
companies.significance: CoWoS-L enables the multi-die packaging that defines AI chip networking topology.

companies.name: Intel
companies.role: Offers EMIB/Foveros packaging as alternative to CoWoS; expanded packaging facility for third-party AI chips.
companies.significance: EMIB provides a competitive packaging alternative, especially for chiplet-based AI networking.

companies.name: ASE Technology
companies.role: Leading OSAT handling advanced packaging and testing for AI accelerator chips.
companies.significance: ASE handles overflow advanced packaging demand, critical when CoWoS capacity is oversubscribed.

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.role: OSAT providing 2.5D packaging services, relief valve for oversubscribed CoWoS capacity.
companies.significance: Amkor's 2.5D packaging provides crucial capacity relief for AI chip packaging demand.

stages.name: Advanced Packaging & OSAT
stages.desc: CoWoS, EMIB, and the packaging that bridges chips to networking

companies.name: Ajinomoto
companies.role: Near-monopoly supplier of ABF insulating film used in virtually every high-end IC substrate.
companies.significance: Without ABF film, no advanced substrate can be built. Single point of failure for AI chip packaging.
keyFacts: Every AI chip substrate needs it

companies.name: Ibiden
companies.role: Manufactures advanced IC substrates using ABF film for AI chip packaging.
companies.significance: Ibiden's substrate quality directly determines AI chip signal integrity and networking performance.

companies.name: Fujitsu
companies.role: IC substrates via Shinko Electric subsidiary. Key capacity expansion for AI packaging.
companies.significance: Shinko (Fujitsu subsidiary) is a top-tier substrate maker alongside Ibiden.

companies.name: Unimicron
companies.role: Advanced PCB and IC substrate manufacturer for chip packaging and server boards.
companies.significance: Unimicron bridges the gap between chip packages and server motherboards for AI networking.

companies.name: AT&S
companies.role: European IC substrate manufacturer expanding capacity for high-end AI chip packages.
companies.significance: European substrate supply diversifies the Asian-concentrated IC substrate chain.

companies.name: TTM Technologies
companies.role: Server and accelerator PCB fabricator handling high-speed trace routing.
companies.significance: PCB trace routing at 112-224 Gb/s requires extreme precision. TTM specializes in this.

companies.name: Rogers Corp
companies.role: Supplies high-frequency, low-loss PCB laminates critical for 112-224 Gb/s signal integrity.
companies.significance: At 224 Gb/s, standard PCB materials fail. Rogers' low-loss laminates become mandatory.

stages.name: Substrates & PCB Materials
stages.desc: ABF substrates, IC packaging materials, and high-speed PCB laminates

companies.name: TE Connectivity
companies.role: Manufactures high-speed connectors, twinax DAC/AEC assemblies, and mezzanine connectors for server boards.
companies.significance: Every GPU-to-switch link requires a physical connector. TE dominates high-speed server connectivity.

companies.name: Amphenol
companies.role: High-speed connector and cable OEM supplying internal harnesses and DAC/AEC assemblies at hyperscale volumes.
companies.significance: Amphenol's scale and qualification with hyperscalers makes it the go-to connector supplier.

companies.name: Luxshare Precision
companies.role: Chinese connector and cable OEM manufacturing twinax assemblies and high-speed connectors for AI racks.
companies.significance: Luxshare provides cost-competitive connector alternatives for Chinese and global AI rack builds.

companies.name: Foxconn Interconnect
companies.role: Builds twinax assemblies, internal harnesses, and high-speed connectors binding GPUs, switches, and trays.
companies.significance: FIT's integration with Foxconn's server assembly gives it unique placement in AI rack wiring.

stages.name: Connectors & Cable Assemblies
stages.desc: High-speed connectors, twinax DAC/AEC assemblies, and internal harnesses

companies.name: Broadcom
companies.role: Provides switch ASICs (Tomahawk family up to 102.4 Tb/s), SerDes/PHY chips, PAM4 DSPs, optical modules, and CPO platforms.
companies.significance: Broadcom's Tomahawk defines the merchant switching standard. Every white-box switch runs Broadcom silicon.

companies.name: Marvell Technology
companies.role: Custom AI silicon, PAM4 DSPs, coherent DSPs (via Inphi acquisition); dominates 400ZR market share and leads in 800G ZR.
companies.significance: Marvell's Inphi-derived coherent DSPs power the pluggable optics revolution.

companies.name: Credo Technology
companies.role: Pure-play in AEC silicon and retimer chips; HiWire AECs widely deployed in AI racks for extending copper reach.
companies.significance: AECs extend copper reach from 3m to 7m, avoiding expensive optics for short intra-rack links.

companies.name: Astera Labs
companies.role: CXL memory controller chips and retimer hubs enabling PCIe 5/6 signal extension on server boards.
companies.significance: CXL enables memory disaggregation, fundamentally changing AI server networking architecture.

companies.name: Analog Devices
companies.role: Analog/mixed-signal ICs for power management, clocking, and signal conditioning in high-speed AI systems.
companies.significance: Clean clocks and stable power are prerequisites for 224 Gb/s signaling. ADI provides both.

companies.name: Texas Instruments
companies.role: Retimer, clocking, and power management silicon used across AI server boards and interconnect.
companies.significance: TI's broad analog portfolio touches every AI server board from power to signal integrity.

companies.name: MaxLinear
companies.role: High-speed PAM4 PHY and DSP chips for interconnect and retimer applications.
companies.significance: Niche PAM4 player that benefits from the 800G optical module volume explosion.

stages.name: SerDes, Retimers & Signal Conditioning
stages.desc: The silicon that pushes bits through copper and optical links at 112-224 Gb/s

companies.name: Arista Networks
companies.role: Leads high-performance cloud Ethernet switching with EOS software; packages Broadcom merchant ASICs for 400G/800G leaf-spine fabrics.
companies.significance: Arista owns the Ethernet switching software stack that most hyperscalers run in production.

companies.name: Cisco Systems
companies.role: Nexus/Silicon One switching platforms plus captive optics (Acacia coherent DSPs, Luxtera silicon photonics); end-to-end IP+optical solutions.
companies.significance: Cisco's Acacia + Luxtera acquisitions give it a vertically integrated IP+optical networking stack.

companies.name: Juniper Networks
companies.role: Data center routing/switching franchise now being absorbed into HPE.
companies.significance: Acquired by HPE. Juniper's switching line merges with HPE's compute for integrated AI fabric.

companies.name: HPE
companies.role: Acquiring Juniper's switching line to offer integrated compute+network fabric for AI workloads.
companies.significance: HPE+Juniper creates a combined compute+networking vendor for enterprise AI deployments.

companies.name: Celestica
companies.role: ODM building white-box switches using merchant silicon for hyperscaler AI back-end networks at scale.
companies.significance: White-box switch ODMs like Celestica capture share as hyperscalers bypass traditional networking vendors.

companies.name: Accton Technology
companies.role: ODM manufacturing cost-optimized white-box switch gear for hyperscale deployments.
companies.significance: Accton's white-box switches undercut branded gear, accelerating the shift to merchant silicon.

stages.name: Networking Equipment
stages.desc: Switches, routers, NICs, and the white-box ODMs building AI back-end fabrics

companies.name: Coherent Corp
companies.role: Formed from II-VI + Finisar merger; major supplier of 400G/800G transceivers, lasers, and optical components; CPO partner for NVIDIA.
companies.significance: Coherent's vertical integration from lasers to modules gives it unique cost and technology leverage.

companies.name: Lumentum
companies.role: Laser diodes, photonic components, and optical circuit switches; positioned to supply external laser sources for CPO.
companies.significance: In CPO architectures, external laser sources become critical. Lumentum is the key laser supplier.

companies.name: Applied Optoelectronics
companies.role: Optical transceiver and laser supplier for datacenter and telecom applications.
companies.significance: AAOI's datacenter transceiver volumes scale directly with AI cluster deployments.

companies.name: Eoptolink
companies.role: Chinese optical module maker with 175% YoY revenue growth, major 400G/800G transceiver supplier.
companies.significance: Eoptolink's explosive growth shows Chinese optics vendors capturing massive AI transceiver share.

companies.name: Accelink Technologies
companies.role: Chinese supplier of finished optical modules and InP lasers/EMLs/receivers for datacenter and telecom.
companies.significance: Accelink provides both modules and critical InP laser components, spanning two layers of the stack.

companies.name: Hisense Broadband
companies.role: Chinese optical transceiver manufacturer serving cloud and datacenter markets.
companies.significance: Part of the Chinese optics ecosystem competing for hyperscaler transceiver volume.

companies.name: Fabrinet
companies.role: Contract manufacturer for optical transceivers and modules, primarily in Thailand; geopolitical hedge as 'China+1' safe harbor.
companies.significance: Fabrinet is the 'China+1' manufacturing hedge — Thailand-based precision optics assembly.

companies.name: STMicroelectronics
companies.role: Co-developing AI-focused silicon photonics interconnect chips with AWS for 800G/1.6T optical links.
companies.significance: STMicro's AWS partnership could produce a silicon photonics platform that disrupts discrete optics.

stages.name: Optical Transceivers & Silicon Photonics
stages.desc: 400G/800G/1.6T optical modules, lasers, silicon photonics, and CPO enablers

companies.name: Ciena
companies.role: WaveLogic coherent DSP family; optical transport line systems; entered pluggable 400ZR market in 2024; ~19% global optical transport share.
companies.significance: Ciena's WaveLogic DSPs power long-haul and metro optical networks connecting AI datacenters.

companies.name: Nokia
companies.role: PSE coherent DSP family plus optical transport systems; acquired Infinera for $2.3B to become #2 optical vendor globally.
companies.significance: Nokia+Infinera creates the #2 global optical vendor, challenging Ciena in coherent DSP.

companies.name: Infinera
companies.role: ICE coherent engine and PIC stack, now part of Nokia post-2025 acquisition.
companies.significance: Infinera's indium phosphide PIC technology was unique in the industry. Now absorbed into Nokia.

companies.name: Macom Technology
companies.role: High-performance analog driver maker positioned to win if LPO removes digital DSPs from optical modules.
companies.significance: LPO (Linear Pluggable Optics) shifts value from digital DSPs to analog drivers — Macom's sweet spot.

companies.name: Semtech
companies.role: Analog driver and signal integrity silicon; benefits from LPO trend shifting value from digital DSP to analog muscle.
companies.significance: Semtech's analog portfolio positions it to capture value as LPO adoption accelerates.

stages.name: Coherent DSP & Analog Drivers
stages.desc: Coherent DSPs for pluggable optics and analog drivers benefiting from LPO trends

companies.name: ZTE Corporation
companies.role: Optical transport equipment vendor; part of top five globally alongside Huawei, Ciena, Nokia, and FiberHome.
companies.significance: ZTE is a major optical transport vendor with strong presence in Asia and emerging markets.

companies.name: ZTE Corporation (HK)
companies.role: Hong Kong-listed shares of ZTE, providing optical transport equipment and networking solutions.
companies.significance: Dual listing provides international investor access to ZTE's optical transport franchise.

companies.name: FiberHome Telecom
companies.role: Chinese optical transport equipment vendor in the global top six by revenue share.
companies.significance: FiberHome serves the massive Chinese telecom infrastructure buildout and expanding globally.

stages.name: Optical Transport & Line Systems
stages.desc: Long-haul and metro optical transport equipment connecting AI datacenters

companies.name: Corning
companies.role: World's largest optical fiber and cable supplier (~4% global share); supplies fiber for datacenter, campus, and subsea builds.
companies.significance: Every AI datacenter needs fiber. Corning supplies the glass that carries the light.

companies.name: CommScope
companies.role: Fiber optic cable and connectivity hardware for datacenter and network infrastructure.
companies.significance: CommScope's structured cabling and connectivity hardware is inside most enterprise datacenters.

companies.name: Prysmian
companies.role: Global fiber optic and armored submarine cable manufacturer.
companies.significance: Prysmian builds both datacenter fiber and undersea cables — two sides of AI connectivity.

companies.name: Fujikura
companies.role: Optical fiber and cable manufacturer; fusion splicer technology leader.
companies.significance: Fujikura's fusion splicers are the tools that join fiber — critical installation equipment.

companies.name: Furukawa Electric
companies.role: Optical fiber, cable, and connectivity products via OFS subsidiary.
companies.significance: Furukawa's OFS subsidiary provides North American fiber supply from Japanese manufacturing.

companies.name: Sumitomo Electric
companies.role: Optical fiber, cables, compound semiconductors, and connectivity hardware.
companies.significance: Part of the Japanese fiber triad with Fujikura and Furukawa, supplying global fiber demand.

companies.name: Nexans
companies.role: Cable manufacturer including subsea and fiber optic cabling.
companies.significance: European cable manufacturer with subsea expertise connecting AI datacenters across continents.

stages.name: Fiber Optic Cable & Connectivity
stages.desc: Optical fiber, cable, and connectivity hardware from datacenter to subsea

companies.name: NEC Corporation
companies.role: One of only three major submarine cable system builders; full pipeline through 2026.
companies.significance: Only three companies can build submarine cables. NEC is one of them, with a full order pipeline.

companies.name: Huber+Suhner
companies.role: Via Polatis unit, supplies MEMS-based optical circuit switches for dynamic fiber rewiring in AI campuses.
companies.significance: OCS enables dynamic fiber rewiring, eliminating the need for fixed optical patching in AI clusters.

companies.name: Vertiv Holdings
companies.role: Sells liquid cooling CDUs, power distribution, and thermal management gear critical for AI rack retrofits.
companies.significance: AI racks run at 70-120kW per rack. Vertiv's liquid cooling CDUs are mandatory at these densities.

companies.name: nVent Electric
companies.role: Profits from enclosure cooling and thermal management solutions required for high-density AI deployments.
companies.significance: nVent's enclosure cooling keeps networking equipment operational in high-density AI environments.

stages.name: Subsea, OCS & DC Infrastructure
stages.desc: Submarine cable builders, optical circuit switches, and power/cooling for AI racks

companies.name: NVIDIA
companies.role: Vera CPU: 88 custom Olympus cores, Arm-compatible. Designed to keep GPU pipelines saturated.
companies.significance: Vertical integration strategy — Rubin racks are sold as 'AI factory blocks,' not components.
keyFacts: Up to 1.5 TB LPDDR5X via SOCAMM

companies.name: AMD
companies.role: Venice EPYC: open rack-scale approach with Instinct MI455X GPUs and Pensando NICs.
companies.significance: Designed to slide into broad x86 ecosystem with minimal friction. Open vs NVIDIA's closed stack.

companies.name: Intel
companies.role: Diamond Rapids: platform bandwidth push with Intel 18A RibbonFET and backside power.
companies.significance: Last stand for x86 defense. 18A is Intel's redemption arc — or its yield-risk story.

companies.name: Arm Holdings
companies.role: Pushing deeper into datacenter host CPU role. Arm-based CPUs could capture large DC share by 2025.
companies.significance: Power and efficiency matter when running AI factories. Arm's ISA enables Vera's architecture.

stages.name: CPU Architecture
stages.desc: The 2026 platform clash — vertical integration vs open ecosystem

companies.name: Dell Technologies
companies.role: Major server OEM integrating AI platforms. Competes with NVIDIA's vertical integration push.
companies.significance: Traditional OEM value may compress toward 'rack assembler' if NVIDIA controls more integration.

companies.name: Hewlett Packard Enterprise
companies.role: Enterprise server and HPC platform provider. ProLiant and Cray systems.
companies.significance: HPC heritage positions HPE for large-scale AI training deployments.

companies.name: Super Micro Computer
companies.role: Specialized GPU server maker with rapid design-to-ship cycles for AI workloads.
companies.significance: Speed-to-market advantage in custom AI server configurations. Liquid cooling early mover.

stages.name: Server Platforms
stages.desc: OEMs and integrators where CPU, power, and cooling collide

companies.name: TSMC
companies.role: Manufactures AMD Venice (GAA-class process) and likely NVIDIA Vera. The shared chokepoint.
companies.significance: A small yield wobble at the new GAA node becomes a big supply shock. Venice is tied to TSMC's first GAA.
keyFacts: Shared chokepoint for all three CPU vendors

companies.name: ASML
companies.role: EUV throughput and uptime define how quickly critical CPU layers can be patterned.
companies.significance: EUV capacity is never 'just add money.' Tools are scarce, expensive, and operationally delicate.
keyFacts: Structural constraint on all CPU vendors

companies.name: KLA Corporation
companies.role: Inspection and metrology central to yield management. Defect detection is the gating function at GAA.
companies.significance: As nodes shrink and complexity rises, defect tolerance collapses. KLA defines what's shippable.
keyFacts: Defect tolerance collapse at advanced nodes

stages.name: Foundry & Lithography
stages.desc: Where the 2026 story becomes a yield ramp story

companies.name: Micron Technology
companies.role: Memory bandwidth is the throttle for agentic workloads. SOCAMM LPDDR5X in volume production.
companies.significance: Agentic AI holds state and moves it constantly — memory bandwidth stops being secondary.

stages.name: Memory Subsystem
stages.desc: HBM, DRAM, CXL — where agentic workloads get throttled

companies.name: ASE Technology
companies.role: Packaging and testing arbiter when demand spikes. Shared infrastructure = schedule setter.
companies.significance: Packaging capacity for CPUs is shared with GPUs — queue competition creates allocation battles.
keyFacts: Shared packaging queue with GPU demand

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.role: Advanced packaging and test capacity. Retains ability to service non-AI server configurations.
companies.significance: Vera-based system shipments can be indirectly gated by packaging queues shared with accelerators.

companies.name: Photronics
companies.role: Photomask maker sitting in the critical path when mask counts rise and defect tolerance collapses.
companies.significance: As nodes shrink, mask quality becomes gating. Mask counts increase with each new process node.

companies.name: Ajinomoto
companies.role: Sole manufacturer of ABF insulation film used in all high-performance CPU substrates.
companies.significance: Near-monopoly on ABF film. Without this single material, no advanced CPU substrate can be built.
keyFacts: Critical for all CPU substrate makers

companies.name: Ibiden
companies.role: Premium ABF substrate maker for Intel and other leading CPU platforms.
companies.significance: Cannot route high-speed signals without Ajinomoto film — but Ibiden turns it into finished substrates.

companies.name: Unimicron
companies.role: Largest ABF substrate maker globally. Supplies substrates for AI chips and CPUs.
companies.significance: ABF substrate capacity expansion takes 18-24 months. Demand outpacing new capacity additions.
keyFacts: Critical for CoWoS and CPU packaging

companies.name: AT&S
companies.role: European ABF substrate manufacturer. Growing capacity for high-performance computing substrates.
companies.significance: One of few non-Asian substrate makers, providing supply chain diversification for CPU platforms.

companies.name: Toppan
companies.role: Japanese photomask leader. Mask counts rise with each new process node, increasing Toppan's content per wafer.
companies.significance: Photomask quality becomes gating at GAA. Defect-free masks are essential for leading-edge patterning.

companies.name: Dai Nippon Printing
companies.role: Major photomask producer for leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing.
companies.significance: Part of the Japanese mask triad with Toppan and HOYA. Capacity concentration creates fragility.

companies.name: HOYA
companies.role: Dominant supplier of EUV mask blanks — the ultra-flat substrates that photomasks are built on.
companies.significance: EUV mask blanks are the foundation of every EUV mask. HOYA's quality defines patterning capability.

stages.name: Substrates & Test
stages.desc: ABF substrates, packaging, and test — where flawless silicon gets blocked

companies.name: Synopsys
companies.role: At the GAA era, EDA modeling accuracy IS yield. Defines tapeout confidence for all CPU vendors.
companies.significance: Every CPU architecture — Vera, Venice, Diamond Rapids — needs EDA signoff. Vendor-agnostic winner.

companies.name: Cadence Design
companies.role: Complementary EDA platform. IP ecosystems matter for coherency and system-level integration.
companies.significance: Design complexity at GAA multiplies verification cycles. EDA revenue scales with tapeout iterations.

companies.name: Siemens EDA
companies.role: Via Mentor Graphics, provides IC packaging and PCB design tools critical for multi-die CPU integration.
companies.significance: Third major EDA player. IC package co-design becomes essential as CPU platforms add die-to-die complexity.

stages.name: EDA & IP
stages.desc: Design tools and IP that define tapeout confidence at GAA era

companies.name: Entegris
companies.role: Filtration and CMP materials that become more sensitive inputs at first-gen GAA ramps.
companies.significance: The 'clean kitchen' layer. If purity drops, fabs don't slow down — they often stop.

companies.name: DuPont
companies.role: Photoresists, CMP slurries, and specialty chemicals for semiconductor manufacturing.
companies.significance: Process materials become more sensitive inputs as the industry moves into first-gen GAA ramps.

companies.name: Linde
companies.role: Ultra-high-purity nitrogen, argon, oxygen, and hydrogen for chip fabrication.
companies.significance: If gas purity drops, fabs don't slow down — they stop. Contamination kills yield.
keyFacts: UHP gas supply to all fabs

companies.name: Air Liquide
companies.role: Specialty and ultra-pure gases for semiconductor fabrication alongside Linde.
companies.significance: Part of the gas duopoly. Gases are a continuous consumable — fabs cannot operate without them.

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.role: World's largest silicon wafer manufacturer. Every chip starts on a Shin-Etsu wafer.
companies.significance: Wafer quality at leading-edge nodes defines what's possible. Shin-Etsu sets the purity floor.

companies.name: SUMCO
companies.role: Second-largest silicon wafer maker. Supplies 300mm wafers to all major foundries.
companies.significance: Wafer supply is concentrated in two Japanese companies. SUMCO duopoly with Shin-Etsu is structural.

stages.name: Raw Materials & Chemistry
stages.desc: Wafers, chemicals, gases — inputs that become more sensitive at GAA

companies.name: Freeport-McMoRan
companies.role: Largest publicly traded copper miner. Direct exposure to the 10M ton supply deficit by 2040.
companies.significance: Price is the only mechanism left to clear the market. Structural deficit = structural pricing power.
keyFacts: AI campus = ~$115M raw copper cost per 230MW site

companies.name: Southern Copper
companies.role: Pure-play copper miner with lowest-cost production and massive reserves.
companies.significance: Long-duration exposure to copper price appreciation with industry-leading margins.

companies.name: BHP Group
companies.role: Global mining giant with significant copper production and expansion pipeline.
companies.significance: Copper is becoming BHP's strategic growth metal as AI drives demand.

companies.name: Rio Tinto
companies.role: Major miner with growing copper operations and Oyu Tolgoi ramp.
companies.significance: Copper production growing as AI datacenter construction accelerates globally.

companies.name: Teck Resources
companies.role: Transitioning to pure-play copper after selling coal assets. Highland Valley and QB2 expansion.
companies.significance: Repositioned as a copper-first company at the exact moment AI demand accelerates the deficit.
keyFacts: Sold coal assets to pivot to copper

stages.name: Commodity Mining
stages.desc: Copper miners exposed to the structural supply deficit

companies.name: Eaton Corporation
companies.role: Provides switchgear, busbars, and power distribution for AI datacenters. Acquiring Boyd Thermal for $9.5B.
companies.significance: Copper is physically embedded in switchgear. Long-lead gating hardware with massive backlogs.
keyFacts: Acquiring Boyd Thermal for liquid cooling

companies.name: Vertiv Holdings
companies.role: Provides CDUs, power conversion, and thermal management for high-density AI racks.
companies.significance: Better positioned for liquid/precision cooling vs pure chillers in the AI datacenter shift.

companies.name: GE Vernova
companies.role: Provides grid infrastructure and power generation equipment for massive AI campus buildouts.
companies.significance: AI campuses need dedicated behind-the-meter generation. GE Vernova builds the power plants.

companies.name: ABB
companies.role: Provides power distribution, switchgear, and automation systems for AI datacenter campuses.
companies.significance: AI campuses require massive electrical infrastructure — ABB builds the grid-to-rack power path.

companies.name: Schneider Electric
companies.role: End-to-end datacenter infrastructure from grid connection to rack-level power and cooling.
companies.significance: Embedded copper in every transformer, busbar, and switchgear panel — pricing power from lead times.

stages.name: Power Equipment Enablers
stages.desc: Transformers, switchgear, busbars — embedded copper with pricing power

companies.name: Quanta Services
companies.role: Builds heavy grid interconnects and dedicated behind-the-meter power infrastructure.
companies.significance: Massive mandated buildout of power infrastructure. The physical builders of AI's electrical backbone.

companies.name: EMCOR Group
companies.role: Electrical and mechanical construction services for datacenter facilities.
companies.significance: The hands that wire and plumb AI datacenters. Skilled labor scarcity amplifies pricing power.

companies.name: MYR Group
companies.role: Specialty electrical contractor focused on transmission, distribution, and commercial/industrial construction.
companies.significance: Builds the high-voltage transmission lines and substations connecting AI campuses to the grid.

companies.name: MasTec
companies.role: Large-scale infrastructure contractor for power grid, telecom, and pipeline construction.
companies.significance: One of the few contractors with the scale and equipment to handle multi-GW grid interconnection projects.

stages.name: EPC & Grid Construction
stages.desc: Engineering, procurement, construction for heavy grid and datacenter builds

companies.name: Coherent
companies.role: Fiber replacing copper trims 10% of metal but signals an explosion in optical volume.
companies.significance: Trade is the upgrade cadence — 800G deployments today with clear 1.6T sampling visibility.

companies.name: Broadcom
companies.role: PAM4 DSP/SerDes and networking silicon powering the optical interconnect stack.
companies.significance: Controls DSP silicon that every optical module depends on. Networking silicon at scale.

companies.name: Lumentum
companies.role: Provides lasers and silicon photonics for optical modules. Fiber replacing copper drives optical volume.
companies.significance: Every 800G link that replaces a copper cable needs a laser. Lumentum makes the light source.

companies.name: Marvell Technology
companies.role: PAM4 DSP silicon that sits inside every optical transceiver. Critical for the 800G explosion.
companies.significance: DSP silicon is the brain of every transceiver module. Marvell competes with Broadcom for design wins.

stages.name: Optics & DSP
stages.desc: 800G-1.6T transceivers and DSP silicon driving the optical explosion

companies.name: Amphenol
companies.role: Makes high-density blind-mate connectors that replace traditional internal rack cabling.
companies.significance: Cleanest hidden alpha — cable-free modular trays create massive market share shift to connectors.

companies.name: TE Connectivity
companies.role: High-speed OSFP/QSFP connectors and cages for next-gen networking hardware.
companies.significance: Every ConnectX-9 and switch board needs precision high-speed connector ecosystems.

companies.name: TTM Technologies
companies.role: Fabricates high-layer-count PCBs replacing traditional copper cable assemblies inside racks.
companies.significance: Cable-free designs move wiring into PCBs. Share shift from cabling vendors to PCB makers.

stages.name: Cable-Free Rack Hardware
stages.desc: High-layer PCBs and blind-mate connectors replacing internal cabling

companies.name: ASE Technology
companies.role: World's largest chip packaging and testing company. Named by NVIDIA in CPO packaging ecosystem.
companies.significance: Advanced packaging revenue >2x YoY on AI demand. Packaging is the real capacity gatekeeper.

companies.name: TSMC
companies.role: Advanced packaging capacity (CoWoS) is where AI chip capacity is manufactured.
companies.significance: Even when wafer supply is fine, packaging turns good dies into shippable systems.

companies.name: Amkor Technology
companies.role: Outsourced semiconductor assembly and test for advanced AI chip packages.
companies.significance: Packaging capacity is shared infrastructure — becomes a schedule setter when demand spikes.

stages.name: CPO & Advanced Packaging
stages.desc: Co-packaged optics and 2.5D/3D packaging value chain migration
